Metabox 2.0: A toolbox for thorough metabolomic data analysis, integration and interpretation

Kwanjeera W

2026-04-02

HOW TO USE

Here are some alternative ways to use Metabox 2.0:

  • Install as a standard R package

  • Use GUI version from Docker

  • Use an online version

INPUT DATA

The input dataset should be a CSV table containing metadata columns along with feature variables, as illustrated in Table 1. Samples are in rows, and variables (features) are in columns. Experimental information—such as batch, sample type, and injection order—is required for IS- and QC-based normalization.

Table 1. Format of the input dataset. This example table includes experimental information (brown), metadata (blue), and metabolite features.

Sample SampleGroup RunOrder Batch SampleType IS_D3-Alanine IS_C13-Phenylalanine L-Alanine L-Valine Leucine
Sample11 DM 14 Batch1 Sample 3415100 2037269 3170319 3053960 918680
Sample14 DM 71 Batch1 Sample 6082869 4748038 5167928 4157701 1654664
Sample16 DM 19 Batch2 Sample 13975619 9542307 12652652 10157293 4233637
Sample20 KF 23 Batch2 Sample 14006790 9625350 7125928 3672381 525327
Sample23 KF 27 Batch2 Sample 6160146 4026638 3203784 2576521 816807
Sample25 KF 29 Batch2 Sample 11925818 10293816 7917620 5083482 2436176

CONTENTS

This tutorial demonstrates how to run Metabox 2.0 as an R package and requires familiarity with R. It walks through five modules. Figure 1 shows the pipeline overview. Follow the script step by step in the sections below.


Figure 1. Overview of Metabox 2.0 containing three key pipelines and five modules

Go to Section:

  • PROJECT INITIALIZATION

  • DATA PROCESSING

  • STATISTICAL ANALYSIS

  • BIOMARKER ANALYSIS

  • INTEGRATIVE ANALYSIS

  • DATA INTERPRETATION

PROJECT INITIALIZATION

1. Setup the project:

  • For this tutorial, download the demo dataset (GCGC_DM_Samples.csv) and save it to your working directory

  • Open R or RStudio, then set the working directory and load the Metabox2 package with the following R command

##### INITIALIZATION #####
setwd("/Users/macuser/projectname/") #set the working directory
library(metabox2) #load the package

2. Load input data

input_dat = read_input_data('GCGC_DM_Samples.csv') #load input file

3. Set Metabox object

Specifying the required arguments for the set_input_obj function to setup the Metabox object:

  • inputdata: dataframe of input data. Samples are in rows and variables/features are in columns.
  • idCol: column index of the sample ID column. Default is 1.
  • classCol: column index of the category/factor column (for hypothesis testing and classification) or the response column (for regression).
  • xCol: column index of the first variable/feature column.
input_obj = set_input_obj(inputdata=input_dat, idCol=1, classCol=2, xCol=6) #setup metabox object
head(input_obj$X) #preview the data

Output: The following output will be printed to the console, along with part of the input dataset. The dataset includes 75 samples across four groups and 91 metabolites, with some missing values.

Data summary:
- 75 samples and 91 variables.
- repeated samples = FALSE .
- class/factor with 4 levels.
- 0 negative variables.
- 478 ( 7 % ) missing values.

DATA PROCESSING

Key steps in this module include missing value imputation, normalization, transformation, and scaling.

Table 2. Summary of normalization, transformation, and scaling in metabolomics.

Step Purpose Correction Methods
Normalization Remove technical bias Injection variation, batch effects IS-, QC-based, median/TIC
Transformation Improve data distribution Skewness, heteroscedasticity Log, square root, cube root
Scaling Balance variable importance Dominance of high-intensity metabolites Pareto scaling, autoscaling, range scaling

Note: In practice, not all steps are required; the choice depends on the data. Users are encouraged to examine their data first.

Missing value imputation

This step replaces missing values using information from the existing data.

Specifying the required arguments for the impute_missing_data function:

  • METBObj: Metabox object.
  • method: missing value imputation method (options: zero, halfmin, min, mean, median, lod, bpca, ppca, svd, knn, rf, default: median).
  • lod: numeric value (e.g., LOD or LOQ) for imputing missing values; required for the lod method.
  • removeall: logical value indicating whether variables with any missing values are removed (TRUE) or retained (FALSE; default).
  • cutoff: percentage threshold for missing values (1–99; default = FALSE). If FALSE, all missing values are imputed; otherwise, variables exceeding the cutoff are removed.
output_imputed = impute_missing_data(METBObj=input_obj, method="rf", removeall=FALSE, cutoff=30)
head(output_imputed$X) #preview imputed data

Output: A summary of the output will be printed to the console, along with part of the imputed dataset. Try other methods or cutoff values if preferred.

Optional: export the imputed data using the following R command

write.csv(data.frame(output_imputed$inputdata[,1:(output_imputed$xCol-1)], output_imputed$X, check.names=FALSE), file='RF_imputed_data.csv', row.names=FALSE)

Normalization

This step corrects technical variation using known reference compounds, quality control (QC) samples, or statistical assumptions about the dataset.

Try IS-based normalization with CCMN method

Specifying the required arguments for the normalize_input_data_byqc function:

  • METBObj: Metabox object.
  • method: normalization method (options: ccmn, nomis, ruvrand, serrf, loess, default: nomis).
  • istd: a column index or a vector of column indices indicating the internal standard(s) (IS) in the METBObj$X dataframe. This parameter is required for IS-based normalization methods (ccmn, nomes, ruvrand).
  • factorCol: a column index indicating the biological factor of interest in the METBObj$inputdata dataframe.
  • batch: a column index indicating the batch information in the METBObj$inputdata dataframe. Required for serrf and loess methods. Each batch must contain at least two QC samples.
  • sampleType: a column index indicating the sample types in the METBObj$inputdata dataframe. Required for serrf and loess methods. Each batch must contain at least two QC samples. Use “qc” for QC samples and “sample” for subject samples.
  • injectionOrder: a column index indicating the injection (run) order in the METBObj$inputdata dataframe. Required for serrf and loess methods. The injection order must be numeric and unique.
output_norm = normalize_input_data_byqc(METBObj=output_imputed, method="ccmn", istd=1:2, factorCol=2)
pcaplot_overview(output_norm, scale=TRUE) #preview normalized data with PCA


Figure 2. PCA of CCMN normalized data

Try Data-driven normalization by median

Specifying the required arguments for the normalize_input_data_bydata function:

  • METBObj: Metabox object.
  • method: normalization method (options: contrast, cubic, eigenms, linear, loess, mean, median, pqn, quantile, sum, vsn, default: median).
  • ref: a row index or a vector of row indices indicating the reference/control samples in the METBObj$inputdata dataframe. This parameter is required for the pqn method.
output_norm = normalize_input_data_bydata(METBObj=output_imputed, method="median") #normalize by median
pcaplot_overview(output_norm, scale=TRUE) #preview normalized data with PCA

Transformation

Transformation mainly changes distribution of the data.

Try Square root transformation

Specifying the required arguments for the transform_input_data function:

  • METBObj: Metabox object.
  • method: transformation method (options: cube, glog10, glog2, log10, log2, sqrt, default: sqrt).
output_sqrt = transform_input_data(METBObj=output_norm, method="sqrt")
pcaplot_overview(output_sqrt, scale=TRUE) #preview transformed data with PCA

Scaling

Scaling adjusts the relative importance of variables (features) so that metabolites with large values do not dominate the analysis, particularly in multivariate analysis.

Try Pareto scaling

Specifying the required arguments for the transform_input_data function:

  • METBObj: Metabox object.
  • method: scaling method (options: auto, level, pareto, power, range, vast, default: pareto).
output_pareto = scale_input_data(METBObj=output_norm, method="pareto")
pcaplot_overview(output_pareto, scale=TRUE) #preview scaled data with PCA

Try observing data with different plots

Metabox 2.0 includes several plotting functions for data visualization, as demonstrated in the following command.

pcaplot_overview(output_pareto, classCol=2, shapeCol=4) #PCA plot

boxplot_overview(output_pareto, plotvar=TRUE) #Variable boxplot
boxplot_overview(output_pareto, plotvar=FALSE) #Sample boxplot

rlaplot_overview(output_pareto, classCol=2, dolog = FALSE) #RLA plot

densityplot_overview(output_pareto$X, plotvar=TRUE) #Variable density plot
densityplot_overview(output_pareto$X, plotvar=FALSE) #Sample density plot


Figure 3. PCA and RLA plots

STATISTICAL ANALYSIS

Metabox 2.0 supports both univariate and multivariate analyses.

Univariate analysis

Table 3. Summary of univariate statistical methods.

Pairwise
ANOVA
Type Independent Repeated Independent Repeated Correlation Linear Modeling
Parametric t.test t.test (paired) ANOVA -> posthoc.test ANOVA -> pairwise.t.test Pearson Linear mixed-effect
Non-parametric wilcox.test/mann-whitney wilcox.test (paired) kruskal.test -> dunn.test or Scheirer Ray Hare.test -> wilcox.test (2W-ANOVA) friedman.test -> dunn.test Spearman N/A

Specifying the required arguments for the univ_analyze function:

  • METBObj: Metabox object.
  • var.equal: a logical variable for pairwise comparisons indicating whether to assume equal variances between groups.
  • ispara: a logical variable indicating whether to perform a parametric (TRUE) or non-parametric (FALSE; default) test.
  • factor2Col: a column index of the second factor in the METBObj$inputdata dataframe. This parameter is used for two-way ANOVA.
  • doposthoc: a logical variable indicating whether to perform post hoc tests for one-way and two-way ANOVA (FALSE; default).

The following commands perform univariate analysis on the demo dataset. A Kruskal–Wallis rank-sum test is applied, as the data include four groups. Users can view plots for L-alanine; change xCol to explore other metabolites.

output_univ = univ_analyze(METBObj=output_sqrt, var.equal=FALSE, ispara=FALSE, doposthoc=FALSE)
str(output_univ) #preview statistical results

boxplot_byF1(METBObj=output_sqrt, xCol=3, factorLv1=output_sqrt$Y) #boxplot by a fator
boxplot_byF1F2(METBObj=output_sqrt, xCol=3, factorLv1=output_sqrt$Y, factorLv2=output_sqrt$inputdata$Batch) #boxplot by 2 fators
interactionplot_byF1F2(x_input=output_sqrt$X, xCol=3, factorLv1=output_sqrt$Y, factorLv2=output_sqrt$inputdata$Batch) #interaction plot


Figure 4. Boxplot and interaction plot for L-alanine

Correlation analysis

Specifying the required arguments for the correlation_analyze function:

  • METBObj: Metabox object.
  • y: a numeric vector, matrix, or dataframe of additional variables (e.g., genes, proteins, or compounds), with samples as row names. If provided, correlations will be calculated between all variables in METBObj$X and y.
  • method: correlation method (options: pearson or spearman, default: pearson).
output_corr = correlation_analyze(METBObj=output_sqrt, method="pearson")
str(output_corr) #preview statistical result
corrplot_heatmap(data.frame(output_corr$corr_data)) #correlation heatmap

Multivariate analysis

Specifying the required arguments for the multiv_analyze function:

  • METBObj: Metabox object.
  • method: multivariate analysis method (options: pca, pls, opls, default: pca).
  • scale: scaling method for scaleC in ropls::opls(): no centering or scaling (“none”), mean-centering only (“center”, default), mean-centering with Pareto scaling (“pareto”), or mean-centering with autoscaling (“standard”).
  • predI: number of components (5 for PCA, 1 for OPLS, NA for PLS for autofit).
  • crossvalI: number of cross-validation segments (default = 3); samples must be ≥ crossvalI.
  • permI: number of permutations of response labels (default = 20).

Try PCA

#Do PCA
output_pca = multiv_analyze(METBObj=output_sqrt, method="pca", scale="center")

Output: The following output will be printed to the console.

Performing multivariate analysis ...
PCA
75 samples x 84 variables
center scaling of predictors
      R2X(cum) pre ort
Total    0.836   5   0

 PCA was performed.

The following commands to explore outputs from the analysis.

#PCA score plot
multiv_scoreplot(METBObj=output_sqrt, score_data=output_pca$score_val, pcx=output_pca$model_summary$R2X[1], pcy=output_pca$model_summary$R2X[2])

#Loading plot
multiv_loadingplot(loading_data=output_pca$loading_val)

#Loding plot of PC1
multiv_loadingplot_bypc(loading_data=output_pca$loading_val, pc=1)


Figure 5. PCA score and loading plots

Try PLS-DA

#Do PLS-DA
output_plsda = multiv_analyze(METBObj=output_sqrt, method="pls", scale="center")

#Score plot
multiv_scoreplot(METBObj=output_sqrt, score_data=output_plsda$score_val, pcx=output_plsda$model_summary$R2X[1], pcy=output_plsda$model_summary$R2X[2])

#Loading plot
multiv_loadingplot(loading_data=output_plsda$loading_val)

#Loding plot of PC1
multiv_loadingplot_bypc(loading_data=output_plsda$loading_val, pc=1)

#VIP plot (Top 10)
vip_filtered = sort(output_plsda$vip_val,decreasing=T)[1:10]
multiv_vipplot(vip_data=vip_filtered, plot_title="VIP")

#Combined VIP and loading plot
multiv_viploadingplot(vip_data=output_plsda$vip_val, loading_data=output_plsda$loading_val, plot_title="Combined plot")


Figure 6. VIP and combined plots

With a given user_input_obj, users can use the following commands to perform OPLS-DA. OPLS-DA works similarly to PLS-DA but is limited to comparisons between two groups at a time.

#Do OPLS-DA
output_oplsda = multiv_analyze(METBObj=user_input_obj, method="opls", scale="center")
#Plots
multiv_scoreplot(METBObj=user_input_obj, score_data=output_oplsda$score_val, oscore_data=output_oplsda$oscore_val, pcx=output_oplsda$model_summary$R2X[1], pcy=output_oplsda$model_summary$R2X[2])
multiv_loadingplot(loading_data=output_oplsda$loading_val, oloading_data=output_oplsda$oloading_val)
multiv_vipplot(vip_data=output_oplsda$vip_val) #VIP plot

BIOMARKER ANALYSIS

Metabox 2.0 applies the MUVR package for biomarker analysis.

For this tutorial, download the demo dataset (GCGC_DM_Samples.csv) and save it to your working directory

input_dat = read_input_data('LC_LN_Samples.csv') #load input file
input_obj = set_input_obj(input_dat,idCol=1,classCol=2,xCol=3) #setup metabox object
head(input_obj$X) #preview input data
output_muvr = run_muvr(input_obj, scale = FALSE, method="PLS") #biomarker analysis using PLS model
MUVR_getvip(output_muvr) #Get VIP ranks
MUVR_plotvip(output_muvr) #VIP rank plot
MUVR_plotval(output_muvr) #Validation plot
VIP rank plot

5 INTEGRATIVE ANALYSIS

Example commands for Integrative analysis module:

input_dat1 = read_input_data('LC_Fat_Tissue.csv') #load input file1
input_obj1 = set_input_obj(input_dat1,idCol=1,classCol=3,xCol=4) #setup metabox object
input_dat2 = read_input_data('GC_Fat_Tissue.csv') #load input file2
input_obj2 = set_input_obj(input_dat2,idCol=1,classCol=3,xCol=4) #setup metabox object
output_mbpls = mbplsda_analyze(data.frame(input_obj1$Y), 
list(lc = input_obj1$X,gc = input_obj2$X),nrepet=10, 
npermut=5, nboot=5, testmodel=TRUE, cpus=1) #integrative analysis
mbplsda_plottestdim(output_mbpls$result$res_optimal) #Prediction error rate plot
mbplsda_plotpermut(output_mbpls$result$res_permut) #Permutation testing plot
mbplsda_screeplot(output_mbpls$result$base_model$eig) #Scree plot
mbplsda_plotboot_vipc(output_mbpls$result$res_boot) #Variable importance plot
mbplsda_plotboot_bipc(output_mbpls$result$res_boot) #Block importance plot
Block importance plot

6 DATA INTERPRETATION

Example commands for Data interpretation module:

Overrepresentation analysis

#pathway, metabolite ORA
output_ora1=overrep_analyze(fnanal_data$compound_data$kegg, nodetype="compound", size=5)
head(output_ora1$enrichment) #preview result
#pathway, protein ORA
output_ora2=overrep_analyze(fnanal_data$combined_data$id[1:9], nodetype="protein", size=3)
head(output_ora2$enrichment) #preview result
#chemical class ORA
output_ora3=overrep_analyze(fnanal_data$compound_data$hmdb, settype="chemicalclass")
head(output_ora3$enrichment) #preview result

Pathway enrichment analysis

#enrichment analysis containing directional values (Fold-change)
output_enr=enrichment_analyze(fnanal_data$compound_data, pcol=5, fccol=6, method="reporter", 
nodetype="compound", settype="pathway")
head(output_enr$enrichment) #preview result
#enrichment network plot by piano package (optional)
piano::networkPlot2(output_enr$network,class="distinct",direction = "down",significance=0.1,lay = 5)
Enrichment network plot by piano package

Integrated pathway overrepresentation analysis

#integrated pathway ORA
output_ora = comb_overrep_analyze(fnanal_data$combined_data, organism = "hsa", size = 3)
head(output_ora$enrichment) #preview result
